---
title: "Notebook36"
solutions-release-date: "2026-09-23"
---

### Setup

Run all of the following before starting the notebook.

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
#| eval: false
! wget -q -nc https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/funs.py
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
#| eval: false
! pip install requests-cache --quiet
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import time

import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

import requests
import requests_cache
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
session = requests_cache.CachedSession(
    cache_name="data/turing/requests_cache",
    backend="sqlite",
    allowable_methods=("GET", "HEAD", "POST"),
    allowable_codes=(200, 404),
    expire_after=None
)

base_url = "https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php"
headers = {
    "User-Agent": "DataScienceBook/1.0 (yourname@example.edu)"
}
```

### Questions

This notebook picks up the Turing Award laureate corpus notebook35 started:
the same 81 recipients of `Category:Turing Award laureates`, the same
`data/turing/requests_cache` cache file, and the same identifier pair
(`page_id` stable, `doc_id` readable). Nothing here starts a second
population; every question below attaches new data to the 81 rows already
defined. That includes rebuilding `laureates` itself in question 1 exactly as
notebook35 did, since a notebook cannot assume another notebook's Python
session is still around, only its cache file. Print results unless a
question says otherwise.

1. In the first block, rewrite `polite_get` exactly as in notebook35. In the
second block, send one `list="categorymembers"` request for `"Category:Turing
Award laureates"` on its own and print `.from_cache` for that single call. In
the third block, rewrite `category_members` exactly as in notebook35, rebuild
the full `laureates` table, and print its shape.

```{python}
def polite_get(url, params=None):
    session.cookies.clear()
    response = session.get(url, params=params, headers=headers)
    response.raise_for_status()
    if not getattr(response, "from_cache", False):
        time.sleep(0.2)
    return response
```

```{python}
params = {
    "action": "query",
    "format": "json",
    "list": "categorymembers",
    "cmtitle": "Category:Turing Award laureates",
    "cmtype": "page",
    "cmlimit": "max"
}
first_call = polite_get(base_url, params)
first_call.from_cache
```

```{python}
def category_members(category):
    members = []
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "list": "categorymembers",
        "cmtitle": category,
        "cmtype": "page",
        "cmlimit": "max"
    }
    while True:
        data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
        members.extend(data["query"]["categorymembers"])
        if "continue" not in data:
            break
        params.update(data["continue"])
    return members

members = category_members("Category:Turing Award laureates")

laureates = (
    pl.DataFrame([
        {"page_id": m["pageid"], "doc_id": m["title"]}
        for m in members
    ])
    .unique(subset="page_id", keep="first")
    .sort(c.doc_id)
)
laureates.select(pl.len())
```

**Answer**: `first_call.from_cache` is already `True`. Notebook35 sent this
exact request weeks ago and `expire_after=None` means it never expired, so
this notebook reaches the network zero times for the population itself; the
81 rows come back from disk in a couple of milliseconds. This is what the
cache-as-raw-layer idea buys across separate notebooks, not just separate
cells of the same one.

2. In the first block, write a small `add_doc_id` helper (a join back to
`laureates`, keyed on `page_id`, with both key columns moved to the front). In
the second block, collect each laureate's outgoing internal links with
`prop="links"`, `plnamespace=0`, `pllimit="max"`, paginating with `continue`
the same way category members did, attach `doc_id` with `add_doc_id`, and print
the shape of the resulting `links` table.

```{python}
def add_doc_id(df, spine=None):
    spine = laureates if spine is None else spine
    return (
        df
        .join(spine.select(c.page_id, c.doc_id), on=c.page_id)
        .select(c.page_id, c.doc_id, pl.exclude("page_id", "doc_id"))
    )
```

```{python}
rows = []
for page_id in laureates["page_id"]:
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "links",
        "plnamespace": 0,
        "pllimit": "max",
        "pageids": page_id
    }
    while True:
        data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
        page = data["query"]["pages"][str(page_id)]
        for link in page.get("links", []):
            rows.append({"page_id": page_id, "link_title": link["title"]})
        if "continue" not in data:
            break
        params.update(data["continue"])

links = add_doc_id(pl.DataFrame(rows))
links.select(pl.len())
```

**Answer**: 27,626 rows. Most targets are places, universities, and other
topics outside the corpus, which is expected; the next question narrows this
to author-to-author edges.

3. In the first block, join `links` back onto `laureates` (matching
`link_title` to `doc_id`) to keep only the edges that land on another laureate,
and print its row count. In the second block, group the result by `doc_id`,
count edges per laureate into `n_links`, and print `.describe()` for that
column. Something about the numbers should look wrong before you even check a
single row. In the third block, use `prop="templates"` on one flagged
laureate's page to find the explanation, and print the offending template's
title.

```{python}
links_internal = links.join(
    laureates.select(
        link_title = c.doc_id,
        to_page_id = c.page_id,
        to_doc_id = c.doc_id
    ),
    on=c.link_title
)
links_internal.select(pl.len())
```

```{python}
(
    links_internal
    .group_by(c.doc_id)
    .agg(pl.len().alias("n_links"))
    .select(c.n_links)
    .describe()
)
```

```{python}
params = {
    "action": "query",
    "format": "json",
    "prop": "templates",
    "titles": "Martin Hellman",
    "tllimit": "max"
}
data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
page = list(data["query"]["pages"].values())[0]
[t["title"] for t in page["templates"] if "Turing" in t["title"]]
```

**Answer**: `links_internal` has exactly 6,480 rows, which is $81 \times 80$,
and `.describe()` shows every laureate links to precisely 80 others: mean
80.0, standard deviation 0.0, minimum and maximum both 80. A real citation
network does not look like this; nobody's Wikipedia article organically
mentions all 80 of their award-mates in running prose. `prop="templates"`
on Martin Hellman's page turns up `Template:Turing Award laureates`, a
navigation box transcluded onto every laureate's article that links to
every other laureate. `prop="links"` reports links from the rendered page,
template included, so the "network" question 1 seemed to be answering was
actually measuring which pages share a navbox, not who cites whom. The
row-count-too-clean-to-be-real check that caught notebook33's spatial joins
catches the same kind of problem here.

4. In the first block, collect the full revision history for every laureate
(`prop="revisions"`, `rvprop="ids|timestamp|user|size|comment"`,
`rvlimit="max"`, paginating on `continue`), parse `timestamp` into a real
datetime, and print the shape. In the second block, group by `doc_id`, take the
earliest `timestamp` per laureate as that article's creation date, and print
the 5 earliest-created articles. In the third block, print the 5 most recently
created.

```{python}
rows = []
for page_id in laureates["page_id"]:
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "revisions",
        "rvprop": "ids|timestamp|user|size|comment",
        "rvlimit": "max",
        "pageids": page_id
    }
    while True:
        data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
        page = data["query"]["pages"][str(page_id)]
        for rev in page.get("revisions", []):
            rows.append({
                "page_id": page_id,
                "rev_id": rev["revid"],
                "timestamp": rev["timestamp"],
                "user": rev.get("user"),
                "size": rev["size"],
                "comment": rev.get("comment")
            })
        if "continue" not in data:
            break
        params.update(data["continue"])

revisions = (
    pl.DataFrame(rows)
    .pipe(add_doc_id)
    .with_columns(
        timestamp = c.timestamp.str.to_datetime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    )
)
revisions.select(pl.len())
```

```{python}
created = (
    revisions
    .group_by(c.doc_id)
    .agg(c.timestamp.min().alias("created"))
    .sort(c.created)
)
created.head(5)
```

```{python}
created.tail(5)
```

**Answer**: 43,103 revisions across 81 pages. The five earliest-created
articles (Tim Berners-Lee, Vint Cerf, Dennis Ritchie, Ken Thompson, Alan
Kay) all date to May through August 2001, only months after Wikipedia itself
launched in January 2001, plausible for names that famous. The most
recently created is Yoshua Bengio's article (2015), years before his 2018
award, since he was already known for deep learning research by then. More
striking are the three articles in between: Edmund M. Clarke, E. Allen
Emerson, and Joseph Sifakis were all created on 2008-02-04, within about two
minutes of each other. All three shared the 2007 Turing Award for model
checking, and that shared award, not a coincidence, is why an editor
created all three pages back to back on the same day the announcement
would have been news.

5. In the first block, collect daily page views for every laureate from the
REST API over the same 2025 window the chapter uses (`all-access`, `user`,
`daily`, `20250101` to `20251231`), skipping any 404 the way the chapter does,
and print the shape. In the second block, sum `views` per laureate and print
the 5 highest totals.

```{python}
from urllib.parse import quote

pv_base = (
    "https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/metrics/pageviews/per-article/"
    "en.wikipedia/all-access/user/{article}/daily/20250101/20251231"
)

rows = []
for doc_id in laureates["doc_id"]:
    article = quote(doc_id.replace(" ", "_"), safe="")
    session.cookies.clear()
    response = session.get(pv_base.format(article=article), headers=headers)
    if not getattr(response, "from_cache", False):
        time.sleep(0.2)
    if response.status_code == 404:
        continue
    response.raise_for_status()
    for item in response.json()["items"]:
        rows.append({
            "article": item["article"],
            "date": item["timestamp"],
            "views": item["views"]
        })

views = (
    pl.DataFrame(rows)
    .with_columns(date = c.date.str.slice(0, 8).str.to_date("%Y%m%d"))
    .join(
        laureates.select(
            c.page_id,
            article = c.doc_id.str.replace_all(" ", "_")
        ),
        on=c.article
    )
    .select(c.page_id, c.date, c.views)
    .pipe(add_doc_id)
)
views.select(pl.len())
```

```{python}
(
    views
    .group_by(c.doc_id)
    .agg(c.views.sum().alias("total_views"))
    .sort(c.total_views, descending=True)
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: 29,565 rows, exactly $81 \times 365$, so every laureate has a
full year of view data and none returned a 404. That is worth noting
against the chapter's own corpus, where a handful of authors have no page
view history at all; this smaller, more recently and consistently edited
population has none of that gap. Geoffrey Hinton leads with over one
million views, ahead of Tim Berners-Lee, Yann LeCun, Marvin Minsky, and
Yoshua Bengio.

6. Plot Geoffrey Hinton's daily 2025 page views with `geom_line()`, save it,
and look at it. Hinton's total comfortably beat Tim Berners-Lee's in
question 5 despite Berners-Lee's article having a 15-year head start and
far more interwiki reach (144 language editions, from question 7, against
65 for Hinton). What does the rest of the corpus already on hand suggest
about why, and what does the plot itself leave unexplained?

```{python}
hinton = views.filter(c.doc_id == "Geoffrey Hinton")

(
    ggplot(hinton, aes(c.date, c.views))
    .geom_line()
)
```

**Answer**: Hinton's own collected text calls him a "Nobel Prize laureate,"
for the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics, a fact recoverable from this corpus
without leaving it, and residual attention from an award that recent is a
plausible driver of elevated 2025 English-Wikipedia traffic even without
Berners-Lee's decades of accumulated fame or translations. What the plot
does not explain is the sharp spike in mid-June, more than 13,000 views in
a single day against a baseline in the hundreds. Nothing in this notebook's
tables says what happened that week, and a rendered line plot cannot answer
a question the corpus was never built to answer; that is squarely the
business of the temporal data chapter, not this one.

7. In the first block, collect `langlinks` for every laureate (batched 50 page
IDs per request, paginating with `continue`), count distinct languages per
laureate, and print the 6 laureates with the most language editions. In the
second block, print the 6 with the fewest.

```{python}
ids = laureates["page_id"].to_list()
batches = [ids[i:(i + 50)] for i in range(0, len(ids), 50)]

rows = []
for batch in batches:
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "langlinks",
        "lllimit": "max",
        "pageids": "|".join(str(i) for i in batch)
    }
    while True:
        data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
        for page in data["query"]["pages"].values():
            for link in page.get("langlinks", []):
                rows.append({
                    "page_id": page["pageid"],
                    "lang": link["lang"],
                    "lang_title": link["*"]
                })
        if "continue" not in data:
            break
        params.update(data["continue"])

langlinks = add_doc_id(pl.DataFrame(rows))

lang_counts = (
    langlinks
    .group_by(c.doc_id)
    .agg(pl.len().alias("n_langs"))
    .sort(c.n_langs, descending=True)
)
lang_counts.head(6)
```

```{python}
lang_counts.sort(c.n_langs).head(6)
```

**Answer**: Tim Berners-Lee tops the list at 144 language editions, well
ahead of Dennis Ritchie, Donald Knuth, Douglas Engelbart, Geoffrey Hinton,
and Edsger W. Dijkstra. The bottom of the list is Andrew Barto and Richard
S. Sutton, the 2024 laureates, and Charles H. Bennett and Gilles Brassard,
the 2025 laureates: their own collected extracts confirm those award years,
and having an award only months or a year old has left translators far less
time to work than Berners-Lee or Ritchie have had since the 1990s. Language
count here tracks time-since-recognition at least as much as raw
significance.

8. A hand-typed list of names, unlike a category listing, comes with typos,
casual capitalization, and titles that belong to someone else entirely.
Write `resolve_titles(titles)`: send one batched request with `redirects=1`,
walk the `normalized` and `redirects` maps until they stop changing (exactly
as the book does for its second corpus), and return each title's resolved
title and page ID. Run it on `"donald knuth"`, `"Edsger_W_Dijkstra"`,
`"Herbert Simon"`, `"Alan Turing"`, and `"Not A Real Laureate Page"`, then
add a column checking whether each resolved `page_id` is one of `laureates`'
own. What happened to each of the five, and why isn't "resolves to a real,
unambiguous page" the same test as "belongs to this population"?

```{python}
def resolve_titles(titles):
    rows = []
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "info",
        "redirects": 1,
        "titles": "|".join(titles)
    }
    data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()

    followed = {}
    for kind in ("normalized", "redirects"):
        for step in data["query"].get(kind, []):
            followed[step["from"]] = step["to"]

    pages = {p["title"]: p for p in data["query"]["pages"].values()}
    for title in titles:
        final = title
        while final in followed:
            final = followed[final]
        rows.append({
            "doc_id": title,
            "resolved": final,
            "page_id": pages.get(final, {}).get("pageid")
        })
    return pl.DataFrame(rows)

titles = [
    "donald knuth",
    "Edsger_W_Dijkstra",
    "Herbert Simon",
    "Alan Turing",
    "Not A Real Laureate Page"
]
resolved = resolve_titles(titles)

resolved.with_columns(
    in_laureates = c.page_id.is_in(laureates["page_id"].to_list())
)
```

**Answer**: `"donald knuth"` and `"Edsger_W_Dijkstra"` each take two hops
(a mechanical `normalized` fix for casing or underscores, then a real
`redirects` pointer) and land back on page IDs already in `laureates`,
exactly the corpus members they were casual stand-ins for. The other three
land on real, live, unflagged page IDs that are not a typo or a missing
title, and none of them belong in `laureates`, for three different
reasons. `"Not A Real Laureate Page"` has a null `page_id`, the familiar
missing case. `"Herbert Simon"` (dropping the "A.") resolves cleanly to
page ID 35817808, a short disambiguation page listing an economist and an
unrelated real-estate developer, the same silent substitution trap
notebook35 hit with John McCarthy and David Patterson, just triggered here
by an abbreviated name instead of a shared one. `"Alan Turing"` is the most
interesting failure: it resolves to a real, single, entirely unambiguous
article, the person the award is named after, who is not himself a
recipient of it, since he died in 1954 and the award was not established
until 1966. A clean resolution with no redirect, no disambiguation, and no
missing flag can still be the wrong page for a population question, which
is exactly why the `in_laureates` check against the corpus's own spine
table, not the resolution outcome alone, has to be the last word.

9. In the first block, rebuild `texts` exactly as notebook35 did (it should be
fast; the cache already has every request) and print its shape. In the second
block, write `laureates`, `texts`, `links`, `revisions`, `views`, and
`langlinks` to `data/turing/` as Parquet files. In the third block, write a
short manifest recording the retrieval date, the population definition, and
each table's row count, and print the manifest.

```{python}
rows = []
for page_id in laureates["page_id"]:
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "extracts",
        "explaintext": 1,
        "pageids": page_id
    }
    data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
    page = data["query"]["pages"][str(page_id)]
    rows.append({"page_id": page_id, "text": page.get("extract", "")})

texts = (
    pl.DataFrame(rows)
    .join(laureates, on=c.page_id)
    .select(c.page_id, c.doc_id, c.text)
)
texts.select(pl.len())
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
from pathlib import Path

Path("data/turing").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

laureates.write_parquet("data/turing/laureates.parquet")
texts.write_parquet("data/turing/texts.parquet")
links.write_parquet("data/turing/links.parquet")
revisions.write_parquet("data/turing/revisions.parquet")
views.write_parquet("data/turing/views.parquet")
langlinks.write_parquet("data/turing/langlinks.parquet")
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
from datetime import date

manifest = f"""# Turing Award laureate corpus

Retrieved: {date.today().isoformat()}
Source: MediaWiki Action API (en.wikipedia.org) and
  Wikimedia REST API v1 (wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1)
Population: direct article members of Category:Turing Award laureates
  (no subcategories)
Pages: {len(laureates)}
Revisions: {len(revisions)}
Pageview window: 2025-01-01 to 2025-12-31 (all-access, user agent class)
Tables: laureates, texts, links, revisions, views, langlinks
Raw responses: data/turing/requests_cache.sqlite
"""

with open("data/turing/MANIFEST.md", "w") as f:
    f.write(manifest)

print(manifest)
```

**Answer**: 81 rows of text, none of it recomputed over the network; the
whole loop finishes as fast as question 1's single cached request did. The
manifest states the same three facts the chapter's own version does for its
own corpus: what was collected, from where, and how much of it there is,
which is what turns six Parquet files sitting in a folder into something a
later reader can check rather than take on faith.

10. In the first block, pull the most recent `rev_id` for `"Donald Knuth"` out
of `revisions`, then request that exact revision's wikitext with `revids=` and
`prop="revisions"`, `rvprop="ids|timestamp|content"`, `rvslots="main"`, print
the returned timestamp and the first 200 characters, and confirm the timestamp
matches the row `revisions` already had for that ID. In the second block,
resend question 1's population request with `formatversion=2` added and print
the type of `data["query"]["categorymembers"]` next to what it was without the
parameter.
Why does pinning a revision ID matter more here than pinning a format version,
even though the callout on API versioning treats them side by side?

```{python}
latest = (
    revisions
    .filter(c.doc_id == "Donald Knuth")
    .sort(c.timestamp, descending=True)
    .head(1)
)
rev_id = latest["rev_id"].item()

params = {
    "action": "query",
    "format": "json",
    "revids": rev_id,
    "rvprop": "ids|timestamp|content",
    "rvslots": "main",
    "prop": "revisions"
}
data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
page = list(data["query"]["pages"].values())[0]
pinned = page["revisions"][0]
pinned["timestamp"], pinned["slots"]["main"]["*"][:200]
```

```{python}
params_v1 = {
    "action": "query",
    "format": "json",
    "list": "categorymembers",
    "cmtitle": "Category:Turing Award laureates",
    "cmtype": "page",
    "cmlimit": "max"
}
params_v2 = params_v1 | {"formatversion": 2}

data_v1 = polite_get(base_url, params_v1).json()
data_v2 = polite_get(base_url, params_v2).json()

type(data_v1["query"]["categorymembers"]), type(data_v2["query"]["categorymembers"])
```

**Answer**: The pinned revision's timestamp matches `latest`'s exactly, and
the returned wikitext opens with the same infobox and short description
`add_doc_id` would already show for Donald Knuth today, confirming
`revids=` really does hand back one immutable version rather than whatever
the live article currently says. `formatversion=2` does not change the
`categorymembers` list's Python type here (both are already lists in this
particular response shape); the difference formatversion changes is in how
`prop="info"`'s `pages` block is keyed, a dict by page ID under the
default and a plain list under version 2. The revision ID matters more for
reproducibility because it names one fixed version of the *data itself*:
without recording it, a rerun of this notebook next year gets a different
Donald Knuth. The format version only fixes the *shape of the response
envelope*; whichever version is requested, the underlying page content is
identical. Losing track of a format version breaks the parsing code;
losing track of a revision ID breaks the claim about what was actually
measured.

11. Between notebook35 and this one, the corpus grew from a page ID and a
title to six tables covering text, links, revisions, views, and
languages, all traceable back to one category listing. Name one place in
this corpus where that population definition (direct members of a single,
official award category, no subcategories) worked in the corpus's favor
compared to the chapter's own novelist categories, and one place a
different population would have changed an answer you printed in this
notebook.

**Answer**: In its favor: `Category:Turing Award laureates` is small and
official enough that deduplication in notebook35 dropped nothing (81 in,
81 out) and every laureate had view data for all 365 days in question 5,
neither of which the chapter's own two-category, subcategory-free
novelist corpus can promise as cleanly. Against it: question 7's
language-count ranking is really a ranking by *time since the award*, not
by importance, because a population defined by one ongoing prize always
has a long tail of very recent members: Bennett and Brassard (2025) and
Barto and Sutton (2024) anchor the bottom of that list for no reason other
than having been added to the category most recently. A population spread
evenly across a century, the way the chapter's own second, hand-curated
corpus is, would not have that same tail, and the same question asked of
it would answer something closer to "who is more famous" than "who was
recognized more recently."
